coredata.dc:title: Leaf-like BiVO4 nanostructure decorated by nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots: Binary heterostructure photocatalyst for enhanced photocatalytic performance
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Chang
Zhang, Xin
Li, Wei
Yu, Yue
Liu, Meihan
Wang, Le
Li, Chen
Zhang, Xinyuan
Li, Xiuying
Lin, Xue
coredata.dc:description: We constructed a binary nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs)/BiVO4 nanoheterostructure in which NCDs coat on the surface of the leaf-like BiVO4 nanostructures. The binary NCDs/BiVO4 nanocomposites showed enhanced photocatalytic performance for rhodamine B (Rh B) and tetracycline (TE) degradation. The NCDs/BiVO4 sample with an NCDs/BiVO4 mass ratio of 0.125% showed the optimal photocatalytic performance. The improved photocatalytic performance of the NCDs/BiVO4 nanocomposites was due to the efficient separation and transport of photogenerated charge carriers induced by fabricated binary nanoheterostructures.
coredata.dcterms:subject: BiVO4
Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
Heterostructure
Photocatalysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: UV–vis DRS, UPS and PL spectral analysis
Degradation of Rh B and TE using NCDs/BiVO4 photocatalysts

National Natural Science Fundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Fundation of China ( 51602006 , 21407059 ) and the Project of Jilin Provincial Science and Technology Development Plan ( 20180520013JH ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the synthesis of the BiVO4/NCDs composite.
TEM image of the as-fabricated NCDs.
SEM images of (a) BiVO4, (b) 0.06% NCDs/BiVO4, (c) 0.125% NCDs/BiVO4, and (d) 0.25% NCDs/BiVO4 samples, and (e)–(i) the corresponding EDS elemental mapping images of 0.125% NCDs/BiVO4 sample.
(a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images of the as-prepared 0.125% NCDs/BiVO4 sample.
(a) XRD patterns of the as-prepared BiVO4 and NCDs/BiVO4 composites. (b) FT-IR spectra of the as-prepared BiVO4 and 0.125% NCDs/BiVO4 samples.
High-resolution XPS spectra of 0.125% NCD/BiVO4 sample: (a) C 1s, (b) N 1s, (c) Bi 4f, (d) V 2p.

(a) UV–vis spectra of BiVO4 and 0.125% NCDs/BiVO4. (b) Plots of (ahν)2 versus photon energy (hν) for the band gap energies of as-obtained BiVO4 and 0.125% NCDs/BiVO4. UPS spectra (c) and the band structure diagrams (d) of BiVO4.

(a) Rh B photodegradation efficiency of different photocatalysts as a function of irradiation time. (b) Cycling runs of photocatalytic degradation of Rh B over the 0.125% NCDs/BiVO4 photocatalyst under visible-light illumination. (c) XRD patterns of the 0.125% NCDs/BiVO4 sample before and after photocatalysis. (d) SEM image of 0.125% NCDs/BiVO4 photocatalyst after photocatalysis.

(a) Trapping experiment of active species during photocatalytic degradation of Rh B over 0.125% NCDs/BiVO4 photocatalyst under visible-light irradiation. DMPO spin-trapping ESR spectra of 0.125% NCDs/BiVO4 for DMPO–⋅OH (b) and DMPO–⋅O2− (c).
Possible mechanism of enhanced photocatalytic activity in the NCDs/BiVO4 nanocomposite.

head.ce:title: Leaf-like BiVO4 nanostructure decorated by nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots: Binary heterostructure photocatalyst for enhanced photocatalytic performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Key Laboratory of Preparation and Application of Environmental Friendly Materials (Jilin Normal University), Ministry of Education, Changchun 130103, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Preparation and Application of Environmental Friendly Materials (Jilin Normal University)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We constructed a binary nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs)/BiVO4 nanoheterostructure in which NCDs coat on the surface of the leaf-like BiVO4 nanostructures. The binary NCDs/BiVO4 nanocomposites showed enhanced photocatalytic performance for rhodamine B (Rh B) and tetracycline (TE) degradation. The NCDs/BiVO4 sample with an NCDs/BiVO4 mass ratio of 0.125% showed the optimal photocatalytic performance. The improved photocatalytic performance of the NCDs/BiVO4 nanocomposites was due to the efficient separation and transport of photogenerated charge carriers induced by fabricated binary nanoheterostructures.

ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductor photocatalysis for the treatment of organic pollutants has attracted global attention during recent decades owing to its efficient photodegradation rate and high mineralization efficiency [1–4]. Monoclinic bismuth vanadate (m-BiVO4) is a well-known photocatalytic material that can be activated by visible light because of its relatively small band gap (approximately 2.40 eV vs. SHE) [5–8]. However, its short carrier transport length and poor charge diffusion ability considerably limit the application of m-BiVO4 [9]. Many efforts have been undertaken to resolve these limitations, such as doping with metal or nonmetal ions, construction of a heterojunction, and surface modification [10–12]. An efficient method is the fabrication of the BiVO4 heterostructure with another compound. Binary composite photocatalysts have been proven to be more effective than single photocatalysts [13–16]. Unfortunately, the inert visible-light-induced composition and broad band gap of semiconductors usually hinder the practical application of composite materials [17,18]. Thus, exploration of efficient visible-light components is urgently needed to build an excellent binary composite system for photocatalytic application [19–24].

Carbon quantum dots have recently attracted considerable attention as zero-dimensional nanostructures because of their low toxicity, high chemical stability, good water solubility, high electrical conductivity, good optical and catalytic properties, and easy functionalization [25–28]. To enhance their photoluminescent properties for improved catalytic application, N, B, S, and P have been added as dopants during the preparation of carbon quantum dots. In recent years, N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs) have been employed as photocatalysts owing to their enhanced properties, and have exhibited satisfactory performance in photocatalytic applications [29,30]. Therefore, coupling BiVO4 with NCDs can generate a binary composite system that can efficiently hinder the recombination of photoexcited charge carriers and improve the photocatalytic efficiency.

In this study, we designed a binary NCDs/BiVO4 nanoheterostructure in which NCDs were added to the surface of the leaf-like BiVO4 nanostructures for the photocatalytic degradation of rhodamine B (Rh B) and tetracycline (TE) under visible-light irradiation. The as-prepared binary NCDs/BiVO4 nanocomposites exhibited enhanced photocatalytic performance compared to the single catalyst BiVO4. The improved photocatalytic performance can be ascribed to the effective separation of electron–hole pairs in the binary composite system.

Preparation of NCDs by simple hydrothermal method. Briefly, citric acid (1.752 g) was added to 17 mL of deionized water and stirred vigorously (30 min) to give a homogeneous suspension. Then, the suspension was added 560 μL ethylenediamine suspension, with magnetic stirring for 30 min. The mixed suspension was then transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined steel autoclave, which was heated at 250 °C for 5 h. The resulting water suspension was then centrifuged at approximately 10,000 rpm and dialyzed in a dialysis bag to remove large nanoparticles. The as-obtained NCDs were kept at 10 °C for characterization and further use.

Leaf-like BiVO4 nanostructures were fabricated via a simple hydrothermal technology reported in previous works [31,32]. NCDs/BiVO4 nanocomposites were synthesized through a facile precipitation route. 100 mg of as-obtained BiVO4 was immersed in 50 mL of DI water. Then, the suspension was added NCDs solution (1 mg mL− 1) and kept the reaction temperature under 40 ℃ to 60 min. After the suspension was filtered, it was washed several times with DI water and dried at 70 °C under vacuum. The theoretical loading amounts for the NCDs were 0.06, 0.125, and 0.25 wt%, respectively. (denoted as 0.06% NCDs/BiVO4, 0.125% NCDs/BiVO4, and 0.25% NCDs/BiVO4) The characterizations can be obtained in the Supporting Information.

The photocatalytic activity of the samples was studied by observing the photodegradation of the Rh B (5.0 mg L−1, 100 mL) and the TE solution (5.0 mg L−1, 100 mL) under visible-light (λ > 420 nm) illumination using a 300 W Xe lamp with a cut off filter (420 nm). In a typical photocatalytic degradation process, 50 mg of catalyst was added to 100 mL of Rh B or TE solution. The suspensions were magnetically stirred in the dark for 70 min (Rh B) or 60 min (TE) before visible-light irradiation. The reaction suspensions were sampled at set time intervals during the photocatalysis process. The Rh B or TE solution was measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer after centrifugation.

The synthesis strategy of the NCDs/BiVO4 composite is clearly illustrated in Scheme 1 . Fig. 1 depicts the TEM image of NCDs, which reveals that the as-synthesized NCDs are mono-dispersed in water solution with average size of 2–6 nm. The morphology of the as-prepared BiVO4 and NCDs/BiVO4 composites was studied by SEM (Fig. 2 ). Fig. 2a shows that pure BiVO4 displays a leaf-like morphology with an average size of several micrometers. After it is loaded with NCDs, the morphology of the NCDs/BiVO4 nanocomposite shows no significant change (Fig. 2b–d) owing to the small size and low percentage of NCDs. Moreover, the formation of the NCDs/BiVO4 nanoheterostructure was verified by elemental mapping of the 0.125% NCDs/BiVO4 (Fig. 2e–i). The maps of Bi–M, V-L, O-K, N-K, and C-K show the same shape and location, demonstrating NCDs and BiVO4 exist in the prepared NCDs/BiVO4 composite. This provides strong evidence of the formation of NCDs/BiVO4 nanoheterostructure. A TEM image shows the leaf-like nanostructure of the as-fabricated 0.125% NCDs/BiVO4 sample (Fig. 3 a). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of the 0.125% NCDs/BiVO4 sample (Fig. 3b) indicates that NCDs are present on the BiVO4 surface. The lattice fringe spacing of 0.23 nm is assigned to the anatase (121) facet of BiVO4 [33]. Further, the lattice fringe spacing of 0.21 nm corresponds to the (100) facet of the NCDs [34].

The phase and crystal structure of the NCDs/BiVO4 composites together with those of pure BiVO4 were investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis. Fig. 4 a shows that pure BiVO4 could be readily directed to monoclinic BiVO4 (JCPDS no. 14-0688). Loading it with NCDs didn’t obviously change the crystal structure of BiVO4. NCDs reportedly display a weak but characteristic diffraction peak at approximately 26.0°; however, this peak was not obvious in the patterns of the NCDs/BiVO4 composites, possibly owing to the small quantity and relatively weak diffraction intensity of NCDs in the composites. Fig. 4b shows the FT-IR spectra of BiVO4 and the 0.125% NCDs/BiVO4 sample. In the latter, the characteristic peaks of C = O and C–O–C are detected at 1456, 1423, 1337, and 1306 cm−1, revealing the presence of NCDs in the composite photocatalyst.

The high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, N 1s, Bi 4f, and V 2p High-resolution XPS spectra of the 0.125% NCDs/BiVO4 sample are shown in Fig. 5 a–d. The C 1s spectrum can fit three peaks with 282.7, 283.3, and 286.6 eV as the center (Fig. 5a), which correspond to CC, CO, and CN3 bonds in the heterocyclic ring of the NCDs, respectively [35–37]. The N 1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into two binding energies, those of triazine rings (C–N=C, 397.4 eV) and tertiary nitrogen [N(C)3, 400.8 eV] (Fig. 5b). The binding energies of Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 are detected at 158.5 and 163.8 eV, separately (Fig. 5c), whereas that of V 2p appears at 516.6 eV (Fig. 5d). Fig. S1 shows ultraviolet-visible absorption and PL spectra of the NCDs. The as-prepared NCDs sample shows an absorption band at approximately 425 nm (Fig. S1a). As shown in Fig. S1b, NCDs emit intense blue fluorescence at about 440 nm by single photon excitation under ultraviolet light. In addition, non-infectious diseases scattered on glass substrates emit blue light by single-photon excitation, which is consistent with the reported results [38–40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis DRS, UPS and PL spectral analysis
ce:section.ce:para: To study the optical absorption performance of the NCDs/BiVO4 composites, UV–vis diffuse reflection spectra were obtained (Fig. 6 a). The absorption onset of BiVO4 is located at approximately 530 nm; thus, the band gap of BiVO4 is determined to be 2.34 eV (Fig. 6b). In comparison with pure BiVO4, the 0.125% NCDs/BiVO4 sample shows absorption in the visible range accompanied by a red shift in the absorption edge owing to the synergistic effect between BiVO4 and the NCDs. The results show that the as-prepared NCDs/BiVO4 composite could be used as a visible-light photocatalyst. UPS spectra of BiVO4 are recorded as displayed in Fig. 6c. The ionization potentials of BiVO4, which is equivalent to the E VB, is calculated to be -7.52 eV vs. vacuum. The E CB of BiVO4 is determined to be -5.18 eV vs. vacuum, according to E CB = E VB - E g. The E CB of BiVO4 is equal to 0.74 V vs. RHE (reversible hydrogen electrode), and the E VB of BiVO4 is equal to 3.08 V vs. RHE (Fig. 6d), Which follows the standard that 0 V vs. RHE equals -4.44 eV vs. vacuum level. As shown in Fig. S2a, the 0.125% NCDs/BiVO4 composite exhibited a considerably weaker PL intensity than pure BiVO4, showing the lower recombination rate of the NCDs/BiVO4 binary composite [41–43], which could be resulted from the formation of nanoheterojunctions at the interface between BiVO4 and the NCDs. The chronoamperometric I–t curves are shown in Fig. S2b. Compared with the single BiVO4, the 0.125% NCDs/BiVO4 sample exhibited enhanced photocurrent responses. The nanoheterojunctions at the interface between BiVO4 and the NCDs can facilitate photoelectron–hole separation and thus retard photoelectron–hole recombination in the NCDs/BiVO4 binary composite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation of Rh B and TE using NCDs/BiVO4 photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: We evaluated the visible-light-driven photocatalytic performance of prepared samples using Rh B dye and TE as target contaminants. Before the photocatalytic reaction was performed, adsorption–desorption tests of the photocatalysts were conducted. Fig. 7 a shows that the adsorption–desorption behavior reached equilibrium after 70 min. Pure BiVO4 showed relatively poor activity (74.3%). However, after BiVO4 was modified with the NCDs, the photocatalytic performance of the NCDs/BiVO4 composite materials were significantly enhanced, and the 0.125% NCDs/BiVO4 photocatalyst showed the highest efficiency (100%) among all the binary composites. The form of the binary nanoheterojunctions between BiVO4 and the NCDs facilitated the separation of photoexcited charges and, thus, improved the photocatalytic performance of the NCDs/BiVO4 nanocomposites [44–50]. In addition, photodegradation of TE was also examined (Fig. S3). The result of the TE degradation under the same conditions clearly demonstrated that the NCDs/BiVO4 composite reveals enhanced photocatalytic capacity than that of pure BiVO4 sample. Fig. 7b shows the measured photocatalytic stability of the 0.125% NCDs/BiVO4 sample. After three cycles, the photocatalytic efficiency decreased slightly, confirming that the 0.125% NCDs/BiVO4 photocatalyst was considerably stable under visible-light irradiation. Fig. 7c was an XRD pattern before and after photocatalytic reaction of 0.125% NCDs/BiVO4 photocatalyst. After three cycles, the positions and proportions of the peaks were essentially the same as those of the fresh photocatalyst. In addition, the secondary 0.125% NCDs/BiVO4 nanocomposite was still intact, further confirming its stability (Fig. 7d).

In this work, three sacrificial agents, isopropanol (IPA), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (DETA-2Na), and benzoquinone (BQ), were employed as ⋅OH, h+, and ⋅O2− scavengers, respectively (Fig. 8 a). When IPA and BQ were added to the reaction system, the degradation rates of Rh B over the 0.125% NCDs/BiVO4 photocatalyst decreased significantly. This result showed that ⋅OH and ⋅O2− play an important role in photocatalysis. However, the Rh B photodegradation efficiency has no significantly decrease after the addition of EDTA-2Na, indicating hole does not play an key role in this photocatalysis process. In addition, 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) spin-trapping ESR spectra were employed to verify ⋅OH and ⋅O2− capture by the 0.125% NCDs/BiVO4 composite during photodegradation (Fig. 8b and c). When the light was on, the characteristic signals of DMPO–⋅OH and DMPO–⋅O2− were clearly detected. Thus, the radical trapping experiments and ESR results demonstated that the ⋅OH and ⋅O2− radicals are the main reactive species.

According to the DRS and UPS characterization results (Fig. 6), the conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) edges of BiVO4 were measured to be 0.74 and 3.08 eV versus RHE, respectively. Based on the above experimental results, this paper puts forward the possible photocatalysis mechanism of the NCDs/BiVO4 nanocomposites (Fig. 9 ). Under visible-light irradiation, the electrons and holes in leaf-like BiVO4 nanostructures may be separated efficiently, the electrons are transferred to the CB, and the holes are left on the VB. These photogenerated electrons and holes generally recombine quickly, and only a portion of the charges could participate in the photocatalytic reaction. Significantly, after NCDs are loaded onto the leaf-like BiVO4 nanostructures to form a binary nanoheterostructure, a mass of the photogenerated electrons in the CB of BiVO4 tend to transfer to the NCDs; consequently, the electrons in the CDs readily combine with oxygen in the solution to form ⋅O2−, and the holes in the VB of BiVO4 react with water to form ⋅OH, both of which are strong oxidizing agents that can efficiently oxidize the organic pollutants [51–53]. The form of the NCDs/BiVO4 nanoheterojunctions facilitates the disconnection of photoexcited charge and thus improves the photocatalytic performces of the binary nanocomposites.

Binary NCDs/BiVO4 nanoheterostructures with different NCDs contents were successfully fabricated. The influence of the NCDs coating on the photocatalytic performance of the BiVO4 catalyst was investigated. The as-prepared leaf-like NCDs/BiVO4 nanocomposites displayed excellent photocatalytic activities for Rh B and TE degradation. The enhanced photocatalytic efficiency was due to the efficient separation of photoelectrons and holes owing to NCDs loading, which resulted in the superior photocatalytic activity.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can inappropriately influence our work, there is no professional or other personal interest of any nature or kind in any product, service and/or company that could be construed as influencing the position presented in, or the review of, the manuscript entitled, “Leaf-like BiVO4 nanostructure decorated by nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots: Binary heterostructure photocatalyst for enhanced photocatalytic performance”.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Fundation of China (51602006, 21407059) and the Project of Jilin Provincial Science and Technology Development Plan (20180520013JH).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.materresbull.2019.110640.
